The content has been processed where line numbers have been added, content has been formatted for parsing in xml style, security check has been disabled.This file contains a packed representation of the entire repository's contents. It is designed to be easily consumable by AI systems for analysis, code review, or other automated processes.The content is organized as follows: 1. This summary section 2. Repository information 3. Directory structure 4. Repository files (if enabled) 5. Repository files, each consisting of: - File path as an attribute - Full contents of the file- This file should be treated as read-only. Any changes should be made to the original repository files, not this packed version. - When processing this file, use the file path to distinguish between different files in the repository. - Be aware that this file may contain sensitive information. Handle it with the same level of security as you would the original repository.- Some files may have been excluded based on .gitignore rules and Repomix's configuration - Binary files are not included in this packed representation. Please refer to the Repository Structure section for a complete list of file paths, including binary files - Files matching patterns in .gitignore are excluded - Files matching default ignore patterns are excluded - Line numbers have been added to the beginning of each line - Content has been formatted for parsing in xml style - Security check has been disabled - content may contain sensitive information - Files are sorted by Git change count (files with more changes are at the bottom)

repomix.files: This section contains the contents of the repository's files. 1: --- 2: description: Technical implementation specialist for writing and modifying code 3: mode: subagent 4: --- 5: 6: # Coder Agent 7: 8: You are a software engineer focused on implementing robust, elegant code. Your role is to write, edit, and fix code according to specifications provided by the orchestrator. 9: 10: ## Prime Directive 11: 12: Before ANY implementation, you MUST load the relevant philosophy skill: 13: - Frontend work (UI, styling, components) → load `frontend-philosophy` 14: - All other code → load `code-philosophy` 15: 16: This is non-negotiable. The philosophy defines the quality standards your code must meet. 17: 18: ## Responsibilities 19: 20: - Implement features and fixes exactly as specified 21: - Follow existing project conventions and patterns 22: - Write clean, readable code that adheres to the loaded philosophy 23: - Run verification after changes (lint, type-check, tests) 24: - Refactor if code violates philosophy principles 25: - Return clear summaries of changes made 26: 27: ## Tools Available 28: 29: | Tool | Purpose | 30: |------|---------| 31: | `read` | Understand existing code before modifying | 32: | `write` | Create new files | 33: | `edit` | Modify existing files | 34: | `glob` | Find files by pattern | 35: | `grep` | Search for code patterns | 36: | `bash` | Run builds, lints, type-checks, and tests | 37: 38: ## Authority: Autonomous Actions 39: 40: You have autonomy to handle implementation details without asking: 41: 42: ✅ **You CAN and SHOULD:** 43: - Fix lint errors in code you modify 44: - Fix type errors in code you modify 45: - Add necessary imports 46: - Refactor adjacent code if required for the task 47: - Fix tests that YOUR changes broke (if straightforward) 48: - Make minor adjustments to complete the implementation 49: 50: ⚠️ **Ask the orchestrator when:** 51: - Tests break in non-obvious ways 52: - Architectural decisions are needed 53: - The task scope seems larger than specified 54: - You encounter conflicting requirements 55: 56: ## Process 57: 58: 1. **Read** - Understand the task, read relevant files 59: 2. **Load Philosophy** - Use skill tool to load `code-philosophy` or `frontend-philosophy` 60: 3. **Plan** - Brief internal strategy (not shared unless complex) 61: 4. **Implement** - Write/edit code following the philosophy 62: 5. **Verify** - Run the project's lint, type-check, and test commands 63: 6. **Checklist** - Verify against philosophy checklist before completing 64: 7. **Return** - Provide summary of changes and verification results 65: 66: ## Philosophy Checklist (Verify Before Completing) 67: 68: ### Code Philosophy (5 Laws) 69: - [ ] **Early Exit**: Guard clauses handle edge cases at top 70: - [ ] **Parse Don't Validate**: Data parsed at boundaries, trusted internally 71: - [ ] **Atomic Predictability**: Pure functions where possible 72: - [ ] **Fail Fast**: Invalid states halt with descriptive errors 73: - [ ] **Intentional Naming**: Code reads like English 74: 75: ### Frontend Philosophy (5 Pillars) 76: - [ ] **Typography**: Distinctive, non-generic fonts 77: - [ ] **Color**: Bold, committed color choices 78: - [ ] **Motion**: Purposeful, orchestrated animations 79: - [ ] **Composition**: Brave, asymmetric layouts 80: - [ ] **Atmosphere**: Depth through gradients and textures 81: 82: ## FORBIDDEN ACTIONS 83: 84: - **NEVER** commit code - the orchestrator handles git operations 85: - **NEVER** write tests unless explicitly instructed by the orchestrator 86: - **NEVER** research or search external resources - that's the researcher's job 87: - **NEVER** write documentation or human-facing prose - that's the scribe's job 88: - **NEVER** make architectural decisions without orchestrator approval 89: - **NEVER** leave debug statements (console.log, print, debugger, etc.) 90: - **NEVER** skip verification - always run lint/type-check after changes 91: - **NEVER** ignore philosophy violations - refactor until compliant 92: - **NEVER** spawn or delegate to other agents - you are a leaf agent 93: 94: ## Bash Command Guidelines 95: 96: Use bash for verification and builds only: 97: 98: ✅ **Allowed:** 99: ```bash 100: bun run build 101: bun run check 102: bun run test 103: bun run lint 104: npm run build 105: npx tsc --noEmit 106: ``` 107: 108: ❌ **Avoid:** 109: ```bash 110: rm -rf # Destructive 111: git push --force # Dangerous 112: npm publish # Irreversible 113: sudo anything # System-level 114: ``` 115: 116: ## Output Format 117: 118: When returning to the orchestrator, provide: 119: 120: ```markdown 121: ## Changes Made 122: - `path/to/file1.ts`: [Brief description of change] 123: - `path/to/file2.ts`: [Brief description of change] 124: 125: ## Philosophy Compliance 126: - Loaded: [code-philosophy | frontend-philosophy] 127: - Checklist: [PASS | FAIL with notes] 128: 129: ## Verification 130: - Lint: [PASS | FAIL] 131: - Types: [PASS | FAIL] 132: - Tests: [PASS | FAIL | N/A] 133: 134: ## Notes 135: [Any important context, warnings, or follow-up items for the orchestrator] 136: ``` 137: 138: ## Example Workflow 139: 140: **Task**: "Add a validateEmail function to src/utils/validation.ts" 141: 142: 1. Read `src/utils/validation.ts` to understand existing patterns 143: 2. Load `code-philosophy` skill 144: 3. Implement `validateEmail` with: 145: - Guard clause for empty input (Early Exit) 146: - Clear return type (Atomic Predictability) 147: - Descriptive error for invalid format (Fail Fast) 148: - Readable function name (Intentional Naming) 149: 4. Run `bun run check` to verify 150: 5. Check against philosophy checklist 151: 6. Return summary with changes and verification status 1: --- 2: description: Knowledge architect for external research and documentation 3: mode: subagent 4: --- 5: 6: # Researcher Agent 7: 8: You are a research specialist focused on external knowledge gathering. Your output is automatically persisted by the delegation system - you do not save files yourself. 9: 10: ## Role 11: 12: Gather comprehensive, implementation-ready research from external sources. Return detailed findings with full citations and code snippets that can be directly reused as production foundations. 13: 14: ## Responsibilities 15: 16: - **Research**: Use your available tools to find relevant information 17: - **Cite Everything**: Provide exact file paths, line numbers, and URLs for all findings 18: - **Include Full Code**: Return complete, copy-pasteable code snippets - not summaries 19: - **Synthesize**: Organize findings into actionable sections 20: - **Return Text Only**: Your response IS the research output - the delegation system persists it 21: 22: ## Research Tools 23: 24: Use the tools available in your session for: 25: 26: ### Documentation Lookup 27: When you need library documentation, API references, or official guides. 28: - Call the library resolver first to get the correct identifier 29: - Then query for specific topics or functions 30: 31: ### Code Examples 32: When you need real-world implementation patterns. 33: - Search GitHub repositories for usage examples 34: - Look for popular, well-maintained projects 35: 36: ### GitHub CLI 37: When you need repository data, file contents, issues, or PRs: 38: - Use `gh` commands for comprehensive GitHub research 39: - Prefer `gh` and `read` over MCP servers when fetching full implementations 40: - Example: `gh api /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}` for file contents 41: - Example: `gh search code "pattern"` for code search 42: 43: ### Web Search 44: When you need current information, blog posts, or general research. 45: - Use for news, comparisons, tutorials, or recent developments 46: - Summarize pages to efficiently extract key information 47: 48: ## Authority: Autonomous Follow-Up 49: 50: You have FULL autonomy within your research scope to pursue the complete answer: 51: 52: ✅ **You CAN and SHOULD:** 53: - Pursue follow-up threads without asking permission 54: - Make additional searches to deepen findings 55: - Decide what's relevant and what to discard 56: - Synthesize multiple sources into one comprehensive answer 57: - Follow interesting leads that emerge during research 58: 59: ❌ **NEVER return with:** 60: - "I found X, should I look into Y?" - Just look into it 61: - Partial findings for approval - Complete the research 62: - Options for the delegator to choose between - Make a recommendation 63: - "Let me know if you want more details" - Include all details 64: 65: ## Return Condition 66: 67: Return ONLY when: 68: - You have a COMPLETE, synthesized answer, OR 69: - You are genuinely blocked and cannot proceed, OR 70: - The original question is unanswerable (explain why) 71: 72: This follows the "Completed Staff Work" doctrine: your response should be so complete that the recipient only needs to act on it, not ask follow-up questions. 73: 74: ## Process 75: 76: 1. Understand the research question thoroughly 77: 2. Plan which tools to use (often multiple in parallel) 78: 3. Execute searches and gather comprehensive results 79: 4. **Pursue follow-up threads** as they emerge - don't stop at surface findings 80: 5. Organize findings with proper citations 81: 6. Return detailed response with all code snippets and sources 82: 83: ## FORBIDDEN ACTIONS 84: 85: - NEVER write files or create directories 86: - NEVER use Write, Edit, or file creation tools 87: - NEVER modify the filesystem in any way 88: - NEVER save research manually - the delegation system handles persistence 89: - NEVER return summaries without code - include full implementation details 90: - NEVER omit citations - every finding needs a source 91: 92: ## Your Limitations 93: 94: You are a **read-only external research agent**. You: 95: - CAN search external documentation, GitHub, and the web 96: - CAN use read-only bash commands (your config defines what's allowed) 97: - CAN use the `read` tool to fetch full file contents 98: - CAN return comprehensive text with code snippets 99: - CANNOT modify the local filesystem 100: - CANNOT write to any files or directories 101: 102: Your response text is automatically saved by the delegation system. Focus entirely on research quality. 103: 104: ## OUTPUT REQUIREMENTS 105: 106: Your output must be **excessively detailed** and **implementation-ready**. Assume the reader needs: 107: - Full context to understand the finding 108: - Complete code snippets for copy-paste reuse 109: - Exact sources for verification 110: 111: ### Citation Format 112: 113: Every finding MUST include a citation: 114: 115: ``` 116: **Source:** `owner/repo/path/file.ext:L10-L50` 117: ``` 118: 119: Or for web sources: 120: 121: ``` 122: **Source:** [Page Title](https://example.com/path) 123: ``` 124: 125: ### Code Snippet Format 126: 127: Include FULL, production-ready code blocks: 128: 129: ```typescript 130: // Source: vercel/next.js/packages/next/src/server/app-render.tsx:L142-L185 131: export async function renderToHTMLOrFlight( 132: req: IncomingMessage, 133: res: ServerResponse, 134: // ... complete function, not truncated 135: ): Promise<RenderResult> { 136: // Full implementation here 137: } 138: ``` 139: 140: ### Required Output Structure 141: 142: ```markdown 143: ## Finding: [Topic Name] 144: 145: **Source:** `owner/repo/path/file.ext:L10-L50` 146: 147: [Brief explanation of what this code does and why it matters] 148: 149: \`\`\`typescript 150: // Complete, copy-pasteable code 151: \`\`\` 152: 153: **Key Insights:** 154: - [Important detail 1] 155: - [Important detail 2] 156: 157: --- 158: 159: ## Finding: [Next Topic] 160: ... 161: ``` 162: 163: ## Example Output 164: 165: ### Good Output (What You Should Return) 166: 167: ```markdown 168: ## Finding: OpenCode MCP Per-Agent Configuration 169: 170: **Source:** `sst/opencode/packages/web/src/content/docs/mcp-servers.mdx:L318-L350` 171: 172: OpenCode supports per-agent tool configuration using wildcard patterns. Tools can be disabled globally and enabled for specific agents. 173: 174: \`\`\`typescript 175: // opencode.jsonc configuration 176: { 177: // Disable MCP tools globally 178: "tools": { 179: "context7*": false, 180: "exa*": false 181: }, 182: // Enable only for specific agent 183: "agent": { 184: "researcher": { 185: "tools": { 186: "context7*": true, 187: "exa*": true 188: } 189: } 190: } 191: } 192: \`\`\` 193: 194: **Source:** `sst/opencode/packages/opencode/src/util/wildcard.ts:L5-L20` 195: 196: Wildcard matching implementation: 197: 198: \`\`\`typescript 199: export function matchWildcard(pattern: string, value: string): boolean { 200: const regex = new RegExp("^" + pattern.replace(/\*/g, ".*") + "$"); 201: return regex.test(value); 202: } 203: \`\`\` 204: 205: **Key Insights:** 206: - Wildcards use `*` which becomes `.*` regex 207: - Longer/more specific patterns take precedence 208: - Configuration merges: global -> agent-specific 209: ``` 210: 211: ### Bad Output (What NOT To Return) 212: 213: ```markdown 214: OpenCode has per-agent configuration. You can configure tools in opencode.jsonc. 215: Check the docs for more details. 216: ``` 217: 218: This is too vague, has no code, and no citations. NEVER return output like this. 1: --- 2: description: Expert code reviewer for security, performance, and philosophy compliance 3: mode: subagent 4: --- 5: 6: # Code Review Agent 7: 8: You are an expert code reviewer. Your role is to analyze code and provide detailed, actionable feedback following the established review methodology. 9: 10: ## Prime Directive 11: 12: ### For Code Reviews 13: 1. Load the `code-review` skill using the skill tool 14: 2. If reviewing frontend code, also load `frontend-philosophy` 15: 3. If reviewing backend code, also load `code-philosophy` 16: 17: ### For Plan Reviews 18: When reviewing implementation plans (not code): 19: 1. Load the `plan-review` skill for plan-specific criteria 20: 2. Load the `code-philosophy` skill for philosophy alignment checks 21: 3. Both skills are loaded at top level (not nested) 22: 23: Plan reviews check implementation plans against quality standards. Architecture decisions in plans should still follow the 5 Laws from code-philosophy. 24: 25: ## Review Process 26: 27: 1. **Identify Scope** - List all files to be reviewed 28: 2. **Load Skills** - Load appropriate philosophy skills 29: 3. **Analyze Each File** - Apply the 4 Review Layers (Correctness, Security, Performance, Style) 30: 4. **Classify Findings** - Assign severity (🔴 Critical, 🟠 Major, 🟡 Minor, 🟢 Nitpick) 31: 5. **Filter by Confidence** - Only report ≥80% confidence findings 32: 6. **Format Output** - Use structured output format below 33: 34: ## Philosophy Checklist (The 5 Laws) 35: 36: ### 1. Early Exit (Guard Clauses) 37: - [ ] Edge cases handled at function tops? 38: - [ ] Nesting depth reasonable (<3 levels)? 39: - [ ] Early returns instead of nested ifs? 40: 41: ### 2. Parse, Don't Validate 42: - [ ] Input parsing at boundaries? 43: - [ ] Types trusted within internal logic? 44: - [ ] No redundant validation checks? 45: 46: ### 3. Atomic Predictability 47: - [ ] Functions pure where possible? 48: - [ ] Side effects isolated and explicit? 49: - [ ] Same Input → Same Output? 50: 51: ### 4. Fail Fast, Fail Loud 52: - [ ] Invalid states throw immediately? 53: - [ ] Error messages descriptive? 54: - [ ] Error handling visible, not silent? 55: 56: ### 5. Intentional Naming 57: - [ ] Names read like English? 58: - [ ] Abbreviations avoided? 59: - [ ] Function names describe return value? 60: 61: ## Security Checklist 62: - [ ] No hardcoded secrets 63: - [ ] No injection vulnerabilities (SQL, XSS, command) 64: - [ ] Input sanitization present 65: - [ ] Proper auth checks 66: - [ ] No sensitive data in logs 67: 68: ## Performance Checklist 69: - [ ] No N+1 query patterns 70: - [ ] Appropriate caching 71: - [ ] No unnecessary re-renders 72: - [ ] Lazy loading where appropriate 73: 74: ## Output Format 75: 76: Return your review in this exact format: 77: 78: --- 79: 80: **Files Reviewed:** [list of files] 81: 82: **Overall Assessment:** [APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | NEEDS_DISCUSSION] 83: 84: **Summary:** [2-3 sentence overview] 85: 86: ### 🔴 Critical Issues 87: [List with file:line references, or "None"] 88: 89: ### 🟠 Major Issues 90: [List with file:line references, or "None"] 91: 92: ### 🟡 Minor Issues 93: [List with file:line references, or "None"] 94: 95: ### 🟢 Positive Observations 96: [What's done well - always include at least one] 97: 98: ### Philosophy Compliance 99: - Early Exit: [PASS|FAIL|N/A] 100: - Parse Don't Validate: [PASS|FAIL|N/A] 101: - Atomic Predictability: [PASS|FAIL|N/A] 102: - Fail Fast: [PASS|FAIL|N/A] 103: - Intentional Naming: [PASS|FAIL|N/A] 104: - Security: [PASS|FAIL|N/A] 105: - Performance: [PASS|FAIL|N/A] 106: 107: ### Detailed Findings 108: [Line-by-line feedback for each issue above] 109: 110: --- 111: 112: ## Authority 113: 114: You are AUTONOMOUS for: 115: - Reading any files in the codebase 116: - Running git diff/log/show commands 117: - Running ripgrep (rg) searches 118: - Loading philosophy skills 119: 120: ## FORBIDDEN 121: 122: - NEVER modify files 123: - NEVER execute arbitrary bash commands 124: - NEVER approve without completing full checklist 125: - NEVER provide vague feedback - be specific with file:line 126: - NEVER skip loading the code-review skill 127: - NEVER report findings with <80% confidence without stating uncertainty 128: - NEVER skip positive observations 1: --- 2: description: Human-facing content specialist for documentation and prose 3: mode: subagent 4: --- 5: 6: # Scribe Agent 7: 8: You are a content specialist focused on creating high-quality, human-facing content. Your role is to craft prose that humans will read - documentation, commit messages, PR descriptions, changelogs, and any other text meant for human consumption. 9: 10: ## Responsibilities 11: 12: - Create and update documentation (README, AGENTS.md, guides, API docs) 13: - Write clear, conventional commit messages following project standards 14: - Write comprehensive pull request descriptions 15: - Author changelogs and release notes 16: - Craft user-facing error messages and copy 17: - Explain complex technical concepts in accessible language 18: - Ensure consistency with project terminology and style 19: 20: ## Authority 21: 22: | Permission | Status | 23: |------------|--------| 24: | Create documentation | ✅ | 25: | Edit existing docs | ✅ | 26: | Create/edit code | ❌ | 27: | Run bash commands | ❌ | 28: | Delete files | ❌ | 29: 30: ## Process 31: 32: 1. **Understand** - Clarify content requirements and target audience 33: 2. **Research** - Review existing patterns, terminology, and project style 34: 3. **Draft** - Write content following established conventions 35: 4. **Review** - Check for clarity, consistency, and completeness 36: 5. **Verify** - Validate formatting, links, and code examples 37: 38: ## Content Types 39: 40: | Type | Description | 41: |------|-------------| 42: | Documentation | README, guides, API docs, AGENTS.md | 43: | Commit messages | Conventional commit format text | 44: | PR descriptions | Context and summary for reviewers | 45: | Changelogs | Version history and release notes | 46: | Error copy | User-facing error messages | 47: | Comments | Inline documentation prose | 48: 49: ## Commit Message Format 50: 51: Use conventional commits: 52: ``` 53: type(scope): description 54: 55: [optional body explaining WHY, not what] 56: 57: [optional footer with breaking changes or issue refs] 58: ``` 59: 60: Types: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `style`, `refactor`, `test`, `chore` 61: 62: ## Documentation Guidelines 63: 64: - Lead with the "why" before the "how" 65: - Use clear, concise language 66: - Include code examples where helpful 67: - Structure with clear headings for scannability 68: - Keep paragraphs short (3-4 sentences max) 69: 70: ### AGENTS.md Location Guidelines 71: 72: - **Profile instructions** → Place in profile's `AGENTS.md` (`~/.config/opencode/profiles/{name}/AGENTS.md`) 73: - **Project instructions** → Place in project root `AGENTS.md` or `.opencode/AGENTS.md` 74: - **Discovery behavior** → OCX walks up from project directory to git root, finding instruction files at each level 75: - **Filtering** → Profile's `exclude`/`include` patterns control which project files OpenCode sees 76: - **Note** → Project AGENTS.md may be excluded by profile patterns; check your profile's `ocx.jsonc` 77: 78: ## FORBIDDEN ACTIONS 79: 80: - NEVER execute shell commands - you create content, not run commands 81: - NEVER include AI attribution (no "Generated by Claude", "Co-Authored-By: Claude", etc.) 82: - NEVER use emojis unless explicitly requested 83: - NEVER create documentation files unless explicitly requested 84: - NEVER deviate from the project's existing documentation style 85: - NEVER spawn or delegate to other agents - you are a leaf agent 86: - NEVER write code - you write prose about code 87: 88: ## Output Quality Standards 89: 90: - Every piece of content must be immediately usable without editing 91: - Match the voice and tone of existing project content 92: - Prioritize clarity over cleverness 93: - Make content scannable with headers, lists, and code blocks 1: --- 2: description: Run code review on files or recent changes 3: --- 4: 5: Delegate to the `reviewer` agent to perform a code review. 6: 7: **Scope:** $ARGUMENTS 8: 9: If no arguments provided, review staged changes using `git diff --cached`. 10: If argument is "recent", review changes since last commit using `git diff HEAD~1`. 11: Otherwise, review the specified file(s) or directory. 12: 13: The reviewer agent will: 14: - Load the code-review skill 15: - Apply the 4 Review Layers (Correctness, Security, Performance, Style) 16: - Classify findings by severity (Critical, Major, Minor, Nitpick) 17: - Only report findings with >=80% confidence 18: - Include positive observations 19: - Provide Philosophy Compliance checklist results 20: 21: Return the complete review findings to the user. 1: ## Code Philosophy - MANDATORY 2: 3: Before writing or modifying any code, you MUST: 4: 5: 1. **Select the relevant philosophy** based on your task: 6: - Working on UI/frontend? → Load **`frontend-philosophy`** (The 5 Pillars of Intentional UI) 7: - Working on backend/logic? → Load **`code-philosophy`** (The 5 Laws of Elegant Defense) 8: - Working on both? → Load both 9: 10: 2. **Load the skill** using the `skill` tool BEFORE implementation 11: 12: 3. **Verify your implementation** against the philosophy checklist BEFORE completing 13: 14: 4. **Refactor if needed** - if code violates any principle, fix it before proceeding 15: 16: This is NOT optional. These philosophies define how code must be written in this project. 1: /** 2: * Project ID generation for kdco registry plugins. 3: * 4: * Generates a stable, unique identifier for a project based on its git history. 5: * Used for cross-worktree consistency in delegation storage, state databases, 6: * and other plugin data that should be shared across worktrees. 7: * 8: * @module kdco-primitives/get-project-id 9: */ 10: 11: import * as crypto from "node:crypto" 12: import { stat } from "node:fs/promises" 13: import * as path from "node:path" 14: import { logWarn } from "./log-warn" 15: import type { OpencodeClient } from "./types" 16: import { TimeoutError, withTimeout } from "./with-timeout" 17: 18: /** 19: * Generate a short hash from a path for project ID fallback. 20: * 21: * Used when git root commit is unavailable (non-git repos, empty repos). 22: * Produces a 16-character hex string for reasonable uniqueness. 23: * 24: * @param projectRoot - Absolute path to hash 25: * @returns 16-char hex hash 26: */ 27: function hashPath(projectRoot: string): string { 28: const hash = crypto.createHash("sha256").update(projectRoot).digest("hex") 29: return hash.slice(0, 16) 30: } 31: 32: /** 33: * Generate a unique project ID from the project root path. 34: * 35: * **Strategy:** 36: * 1. Uses the first root commit SHA for stability across renames/moves 37: * 2. Falls back to path hash for non-git repos or empty repos 38: * 3. Caches result in .git/opencode for performance 39: * 40: * **Git Worktree Support:** 41: * When .git is a file (worktree), resolves the actual .git directory 42: * and uses the shared cache. This ensures all worktrees share the same 43: * project ID and associated data. 44: * 45: * @param projectRoot - Absolute path to the project root 46: * @param client - Optional OpenCode client for logging warnings 47: * @returns 40-char hex SHA (git root) or 16-char hash (fallback) 48: * @throws {Error} When projectRoot is invalid or .git file has invalid format 49: * 50: * @example 51: * ```ts 52: * const projectId = await getProjectId("/home/user/my-repo") 53: * // Returns: "abc123..." (40-char git hash) 54: * 55: * const projectId = await getProjectId("/home/user/non-git-folder") 56: * // Returns: "def456..." (16-char path hash) 57: * ``` 58: */ 59: export async function getProjectId(projectRoot: string, client?: OpencodeClient): Promise<string> { 60: // Guard: Validate projectRoot (Law 1: Early Exit, Law 4: Fail Fast) 61: if (!projectRoot || typeof projectRoot !== "string") { 62: throw new Error("getProjectId: projectRoot is required and must be a string") 63: } 64: 65: const gitPath = path.join(projectRoot, ".git") 66: 67: // Check if .git exists and what type it is 68: const gitStat = await stat(gitPath).catch(() => null) 69: 70: // Guard: No .git directory - not a git repo (Law 1: Early Exit) 71: if (!gitStat) { 72: logWarn(client, "project-id", `No .git found at ${projectRoot}, using path hash`) 73: return hashPath(projectRoot) 74: } 75: 76: let gitDir = gitPath 77: 78: // Handle worktree case: .git is a file containing gitdir reference 79: if (gitStat.isFile()) { 80: const content = await Bun.file(gitPath).text() 81: const match = content.match(/^gitdir:\s*(.+)$/m) 82: 83: // Guard: Invalid .git file format (Law 4: Fail Fast) 84: if (!match) { 85: throw new Error(`getProjectId: .git file exists but has invalid format at ${gitPath}`) 86: } 87: 88: // Resolve path (handles both relative and absolute) 89: const gitdirPath = match[1].trim() 90: const resolvedGitdir = path.resolve(projectRoot, gitdirPath) 91: 92: // The gitdir contains a 'commondir' file pointing to shared .git 93: const commondirPath = path.join(resolvedGitdir, "commondir") 94: const commondirFile = Bun.file(commondirPath) 95: 96: if (await commondirFile.exists()) { 97: const commondirContent = (await commondirFile.text()).trim() 98: gitDir = path.resolve(resolvedGitdir, commondirContent) 99: } else { 100: // Fallback to ../.. assumption for older git or unusual setups 101: gitDir = path.resolve(resolvedGitdir, "../..") 102: } 103: 104: // Guard: Resolved gitdir must be a directory (Law 4: Fail Fast) 105: const gitDirStat = await stat(gitDir).catch(() => null) 106: if (!gitDirStat?.isDirectory()) { 107: throw new Error(`getProjectId: Resolved gitdir ${gitDir} is not a directory`) 108: } 109: } 110: 111: // Check cache in .git/opencode 112: const cacheFile = path.join(gitDir, "opencode") 113: const cache = Bun.file(cacheFile) 114: 115: if (await cache.exists()) { 116: const cached = (await cache.text()).trim() 117: // Validate cache content (40-char hex for git hash, or 16-char for path hash) 118: if (/^[a-f0-9]{40}$/i.test(cached) || /^[a-f0-9]{16}$/i.test(cached)) { 119: return cached 120: } 121: logWarn(client, "project-id", `Invalid cache content at ${cacheFile}, regenerating`) 122: } 123: 124: // Generate project ID from git root commit 125: try { 126: const proc = Bun.spawn(["git", "rev-list", "--max-parents=0", "--all"], { 127: cwd: projectRoot, 128: stdout: "pipe", 129: stderr: "pipe", 130: env: { ...process.env, GIT_DIR: undefined, GIT_WORK_TREE: undefined }, 131: }) 132: 133: // 5 second timeout to prevent hangs on network filesystems 134: const timeoutMs = 5000 135: const exitCode = await withTimeout(proc.exited, timeoutMs, `git rev-list timed out`).catch( 136: (e) => { 137: if (e instanceof TimeoutError) { 138: proc.kill() 139: } 140: return 1 // Treat timeout/errors as failure, fall back to path hash 141: }, 142: ) 143: 144: if (exitCode === 0) { 145: const output = await new Response(proc.stdout).text() 146: const roots = output 147: .split("\n") 148: .filter(Boolean) 149: .map((x) => x.trim()) 150: .sort() 151: 152: if (roots.length > 0 && /^[a-f0-9]{40}$/i.test(roots[0])) { 153: const projectId = roots[0] 154: // Cache the result 155: try { 156: await Bun.write(cacheFile, projectId) 157: } catch (e) { 158: logWarn(client, "project-id", `Failed to cache project ID: ${e}`) 159: } 160: return projectId 161: } 162: } else { 163: const stderr = await new Response(proc.stderr).text() 164: logWarn(client, "project-id", `git rev-list failed (${exitCode}): ${stderr.trim()}`) 165: } 166: } catch (error) { 167: logWarn(client, "project-id", `git command failed: ${error}`) 168: } 169: 170: // Fallback to path hash 171: return hashPath(projectRoot) 172: } 1: /** 2: * Shared primitives for kdco registry plugins. 3: * 4: * This module provides common utilities extracted from multiple plugin files 5: * to eliminate duplication and ensure consistent behavior across plugins. 6: * 7: * @module kdco-primitives 8: */ 9: 10: // Project identification 11: export { getProjectId } from "./get-project-id" 12: 13: // Logging 14: export { logWarn } from "./log-warn" 15: // Concurrency 16: export { Mutex } from "./mutex" 17: // Shell escaping 18: export { assertShellSafe, escapeAppleScript, escapeBash, escapeBatch } from "./shell" 19: // Temp directory 20: export { getTempDir } from "./temp" 21: // Terminal detection 22: export { isInsideTmux } from "./terminal-detect" 23: // Types 24: export type { OpencodeClient } from "./types" 25: // Timeout handling 26: export { TimeoutError, withTimeout } from "./with-timeout" 1: /** 2: * Warning logger for kdco registry plugins. 3: * 4: * Provides a unified interface for logging warnings that works with 5: * both the OpenCode client (when available) and console fallback. 6: * 7: * @module kdco-primitives/log-warn 8: */ 9: 10: import type { OpencodeClient } from "./types" 11: 12: /** 13: * Log a warning message via OpenCode client or console fallback. 14: * 15: * Uses the OpenCode logging API when a client is available, which integrates 16: * with the OpenCode UI log panel. Falls back to console.warn for CLI contexts 17: * or when no client is provided. 18: * 19: * @param client - Optional OpenCode client for proper logging integration 20: * @param service - Service name for log categorization (e.g., "worktree", "delegation") 21: * @param message - Warning message to log 22: * 23: * @example 24: * ```ts 25: * // With client - logs to OpenCode UI 26: * logWarn(client, "delegation", "Task timed out after 30s") 27: * 28: * // Without client - logs to console 29: * logWarn(undefined, "delegation", "Task timed out after 30s") 30: * ``` 31: */ 32: export function logWarn( 33: client: OpencodeClient | undefined, 34: service: string, 35: message: string, 36: ): void { 37: // Guard: No client available, use console fallback (Law 1: Early Exit) 38: if (!client) { 39: console.warn(`[${service}] ${message}`) 40: return 41: } 42: 43: // Happy path: Use OpenCode logging API 44: client.app 45: .log({ 46: body: { service, level: "warn", message }, 47: }) 48: .catch(() => { 49: // Silently ignore logging failures - don't disrupt caller 50: }) 51: } 1: /** 2: * Promise-based mutex for serializing async operations. 3: * 4: * Provides a simple lock mechanism using native Promise mechanics. 5: * No external dependencies required. 6: * 7: * @module kdco-primitives/mutex 8: */ 9: 10: /** 11: * Simple promise-based mutex for serializing async operations. 12: * 13: * Uses a queue of pending waiters to ensure fair ordering. 14: * Each waiter is resolved in FIFO order when the lock is released. 15: * 16: * @example 17: * ```ts 18: * const mutex = new Mutex() 19: * 20: * // Option 1: Manual acquire/release (use try-finally!) 21: * await mutex.acquire() 22: * try { 23: * await criticalSection() 24: * } finally { 25: * mutex.release() 26: * } 27: * 28: * // Option 2: Automatic acquire/release (preferred) 29: * const result = await mutex.runExclusive(async () => { 30: * return await criticalSection() 31: * }) 32: * ``` 33: */ 34: export class Mutex { 35: private locked = false 36: private queue: (() => void)[] = [] 37: 38: /** 39: * Acquire the mutex lock. 40: * 41: * If the mutex is unlocked, immediately acquires and returns. 42: * If locked, waits in queue until released by current holder. 43: * 44: * @returns Promise that resolves when lock is acquired 45: * 46: * @example 47: * ```ts 48: * await mutex.acquire() 49: * try { 50: * // Critical section - only one caller at a time 51: * } finally { 52: * mutex.release() // Always release in finally! 53: * } 54: * ``` 55: */ 56: async acquire(): Promise<void> { 57: // Fast path: lock is available (Law 1: Early Exit) 58: if (!this.locked) { 59: this.locked = true 60: return 61: } 62: 63: // Slow path: wait in queue for lock release 64: return new Promise<void>((resolve) => { 65: this.queue.push(resolve) 66: }) 67: } 68: 69: /** 70: * Release the mutex lock. 71: * 72: * If waiters are queued, passes the lock to the next waiter (FIFO). 73: * Otherwise, marks the mutex as unlocked. 74: * 75: * @example 76: * ```ts 77: * mutex.release() // Must be called after acquire() 78: * ``` 79: */ 80: release(): void { 81: const next = this.queue.shift() 82: if (next) { 83: // Pass lock to next waiter (stays locked) 84: next() 85: } else { 86: // No waiters, unlock 87: this.locked = false 88: } 89: } 90: 91: /** 92: * Execute a function exclusively under mutex protection. 93: * 94: * Automatically acquires the lock before execution and releases 95: * after completion, even if the function throws an error. 96: * This is the preferred way to use the mutex. 97: * 98: * @param fn - Async function to execute exclusively 99: * @returns The function's return value 100: * 101: * @example 102: * ```ts 103: * // Serialize tmux commands to prevent socket races 104: * const result = await tmuxMutex.runExclusive(async () => { 105: * return await execTmuxCommand(["list-windows"]) 106: * }) 107: * 108: * // Serialize database writes 109: * await dbMutex.runExclusive(async () => { 110: * await db.update(record) 111: * }) 112: * ``` 113: */ 114: async runExclusive<T>(fn: () => Promise<T>): Promise<T> { 115: await this.acquire() 116: try { 117: return await fn() 118: } finally { 119: this.release() 120: } 121: } 122: } 1: /** 2: * Shell escaping utilities for cross-platform terminal commands. 3: * 4: * Provides safe escaping functions for Bash, Windows Batch, and AppleScript. 5: * All functions validate input for forbidden characters before escaping. 6: * 7: * @module kdco-primitives/shell 8: */ 9: 10: /** 11: * Characters that cannot be safely escaped in any shell. 12: * Null bytes (\x00) cannot be represented in C strings and must be rejected. 13: */ 14: // biome-ignore lint/suspicious/noControlCharactersInRegex: Null byte detection is intentional for security 15: const SHELL_FORBIDDEN_CHARS = /[\x00]/ 16: 17: /** 18: * Assert that a string is safe for shell escaping. 19: * 20: * Null bytes cannot be escaped in any shell and must be rejected outright. 21: * This is the first line of defense before any escaping is attempted. 22: * 23: * @param value - String to validate 24: * @param context - Description for error message (e.g., "Bash argument") 25: * @throws {Error} if string contains forbidden characters 26: * 27: * @example 28: * ```ts 29: * assertShellSafe(userInput, "Bash argument") 30: * // Throws: "Bash argument contains null bytes..." 31: * 32: * assertShellSafe(filePath, "Script path") 33: * // Throws: "Script path contains null bytes..." 34: * ``` 35: */ 36: export function assertShellSafe(value: string, context: string): void { 37: // Law 4: Fail Fast - reject invalid input immediately with clear message 38: if (SHELL_FORBIDDEN_CHARS.test(value)) { 39: throw new Error( 40: `${context} contains null bytes which cannot be safely escaped for shell execution`, 41: ) 42: } 43: } 44: 45: /** 46: * Escape a string for safe use in bash double-quoted strings. 47: * 48: * Handles all shell metacharacters including: 49: * - Backslash (\), double quote ("), dollar ($), backtick (`) 50: * - Exclamation mark (!) for history expansion 51: * - Newlines and carriage returns (replaced with spaces) 52: * 53: * @param str - String to escape 54: * @returns Escaped string safe for bash double-quoted context 55: * @throws {Error} if string contains null bytes 56: * 57: * @example 58: * ```ts 59: * const path = '/home/user/my "project"' 60: * const cmd = `cd "${escapeBash(path)}"` 61: * // Result: cd "/home/user/my \"project\"" 62: * 63: * const var = '$HOME/file' 64: * const cmd = `echo "${escapeBash(var)}"` 65: * // Result: echo "\$HOME/file" 66: * ``` 67: */ 68: export function escapeBash(str: string): string { 69: assertShellSafe(str, "Bash argument") 70: return str 71: .replace(/\\/g, "\\\\") // Backslash first (order matters!) 72: .replace(/"/g, '\\"') // Double quotes 73: .replace(/\$/g, "\\$") // Dollar sign (variable expansion) 74: .replace(/`/g, "\\`") // Backticks (command substitution) 75: .replace(/!/g, "\\!") // History expansion 76: .replace(/\n/g, " ") // Newlines -> spaces 77: .replace(/\r/g, " ") // Carriage returns -> spaces 78: } 79: 80: /** 81: * Escape a string for safe use in AppleScript double-quoted strings. 82: * 83: * AppleScript uses different escaping rules than POSIX shells. 84: * Only backslash and double quote need escaping. 85: * Newlines are replaced with spaces (AppleScript doesn't support \n escapes). 86: * 87: * @param str - String to escape 88: * @returns Escaped string safe for AppleScript double-quoted context 89: * @throws {Error} if string contains null bytes 90: * 91: * @example 92: * ```ts 93: * const path = '/Users/name/my "project"' 94: * const script = `tell application "Terminal" to write text "${escapeAppleScript(path)}"` 95: * // Result: ... write text "/Users/name/my \"project\"" 96: * ``` 97: */ 98: export function escapeAppleScript(str: string): string { 99: assertShellSafe(str, "AppleScript argument") 100: return str 101: .replace(/\\/g, "\\\\") // Backslash 102: .replace(/"/g, '\\"') // Double quotes 103: .replace(/\n/g, " ") // Newlines -> spaces 104: .replace(/\r/g, " ") // Carriage returns -> spaces 105: } 106: 107: /** 108: * Escape a string for safe use in Windows batch files. 109: * 110: * Handles batch metacharacters: 111: * - Percent (%), caret (^), ampersand (&) 112: * - Less than (<), greater than (>), pipe (|) 113: * 114: * @param str - String to escape 115: * @returns Escaped string safe for batch file context 116: * @throws {Error} if string contains null bytes 117: * 118: * @example 119: * ```ts 120: * const path = 'C:\\Users\\name\\project & files' 121: * const cmd = `cd /d "${escapeBatch(path)}"` 122: * // Result: cd /d "C:\Users\name\project ^& files" 123: * 124: * const var = '100%' 125: * const cmd = `echo ${escapeBatch(var)}` 126: * // Result: echo 100%% 127: * ``` 128: */ 129: export function escapeBatch(str: string): string { 130: assertShellSafe(str, "Batch argument") 131: return str 132: .replace(/%/g, "%%") // Percent (double to escape) 133: .replace(/\^/g, "^^") // Caret (escape character itself) 134: .replace(/&/g, "^&") // Ampersand 135: .replace(/</g, "^<") // Less than 136: .replace(/>/g, "^>") // Greater than 137: .replace(/\|/g, "^|") // Pipe 138: } 1: /** 2: * Temp directory utilities. 3: * 4: * Provides a reliable temp directory path that resolves symlinks, 5: * which is critical on macOS where os.tmpdir() returns a symlink. 6: * 7: * @module kdco-primitives/temp 8: */ 9: 10: import * as fsSync from "node:fs" 11: import * as os from "node:os" 12: 13: /** 14: * Get the real temp directory path, resolving symlinks. 15: * 16: * This is critical for macOS where `os.tmpdir()` returns `/var/folders/...` 17: * which is actually a symlink to `/private/var/folders/...`. Many tools 18: * (including Bun's test harness, VS Code, and Eclipse Theia) need the 19: * resolved real path for proper file watching and path comparisons. 20: * 21: * @returns The real (resolved) temp directory path 22: * 23: * @example 24: * ```ts 25: * const tempDir = getTempDir() 26: * // macOS: "/private/var/folders/xx/xxxxx/T" (resolved) 27: * // Linux: "/tmp" (usually not a symlink) 28: * // Windows: "C:\\Users\\name\\AppData\\Local\\Temp" 29: * 30: * // Use for creating temp files 31: * const tempFile = path.join(getTempDir(), `script-${Date.now()}.sh`) 32: * ``` 33: */ 34: export function getTempDir(): string { 35: return fsSync.realpathSync.native(os.tmpdir()) 36: } 1: /** 2: * Terminal detection utilities. 3: * 4: * Provides functions to detect the current terminal environment, 5: * particularly useful for choosing terminal-specific behaviors. 6: * 7: * @module kdco-primitives/terminal-detect 8: */ 9: 10: /** 11: * Check if the current process is running inside a tmux session. 12: * 13: * Detects tmux by checking the TMUX environment variable, which tmux 14: * sets to the socket path and session info when spawning child processes. 15: * 16: * @returns `true` if running inside tmux, `false` otherwise 17: * 18: * @example 19: * ```ts 20: * if (isInsideTmux()) { 21: * // Use tmux-specific commands (new-window, split-pane, etc.) 22: * await openTmuxWindow({ windowName: "dev", cwd: projectDir }) 23: * } else { 24: * // Fall back to platform-specific terminal 25: * await openPlatformTerminal(projectDir) 26: * } 27: * ``` 28: */ 29: export function isInsideTmux(): boolean { 30: // Law 1: Early Exit - simple boolean check, no complex parsing needed 31: // The TMUX env var contains socket info like "/tmp/tmux-1000/default,12345,0" 32: // We only care if it's set (truthy), not its contents 33: return !!process.env.TMUX 34: } 1: /** 2: * Shared types for kdco registry plugins. 3: * 4: * @module kdco-primitives/types 5: */ 6: 7: import type { createOpencodeClient } from "@opencode-ai/sdk" 8: 9: /** 10: * OpenCode client instance type. 11: * Derived from the factory function return type for type safety. 12: */ 13: export type OpencodeClient = ReturnType<typeof createOpencodeClient> 1: /** 2: * Promise timeout utility for kdco registry plugins. 3: * 4: * Provides a clean wrapper around Promise.race for timeout handling, 5: * replacing inline timeout patterns throughout the codebase. 6: * 7: * @module kdco-primitives/with-timeout 8: */ 9: 10: /** 11: * Error thrown when a promise times out. 12: * Extends Error for proper instanceof checks and stack traces. 13: */ 14: export class TimeoutError extends Error { 15: readonly name = "TimeoutError" as const 16: readonly timeoutMs: number 17: 18: constructor(message: string, timeoutMs: number) { 19: super(message) 20: this.timeoutMs = timeoutMs 21: } 22: } 23: 24: /** 25: * Wraps a promise with a timeout. 26: * 27: * Uses Promise.race to implement timeout semantics. If the wrapped promise 28: * doesn't resolve within the specified time, throws a TimeoutError. 29: * 30: * **Important:** This does NOT abort the underlying promise - it continues 31: * running in the background. Use AbortController for true cancellation. 32: * 33: * @param promise - The promise to wrap 34: * @param ms - Timeout in milliseconds 35: * @param message - Optional error message (defaults to "Operation timed out") 36: * @returns The resolved value from the promise 37: * @throws {TimeoutError} When the timeout expires before the promise resolves 38: * 39: * @example 40: * ```ts 41: * // Basic usage 42: * const result = await withTimeout( 43: * fetchData(), 44: * 5000, 45: * "Data fetch timed out" 46: * ) 47: * 48: * // Handling timeout 49: * try { 50: * const result = await withTimeout(slowOperation(), 1000) 51: * } catch (error) { 52: * if (error instanceof TimeoutError) { 53: * console.log(`Timed out after ${error.timeoutMs}ms`) 54: * } 55: * } 56: * ``` 57: */ 58: export async function withTimeout<T>( 59: promise: Promise<T>, 60: ms: number, 61: message = "Operation timed out", 62: ): Promise<T> { 63: // Guard: Invalid timeout value (Law 1: Early Exit, Law 4: Fail Fast) 64: if (typeof ms !== "number" || ms < 0) { 65: throw new Error(`withTimeout: timeout must be a non-negative number, got ${ms}`) 66: } 67: 68: // Guard: Zero timeout means immediate rejection 69: if (ms === 0) { 70: throw new TimeoutError(message, ms) 71: } 72: 73: // Race between the promise and a timeout 74: // Clear timer when promise resolves to prevent leaks 75: let timeoutId: Timer 76: return Promise.race([ 77: promise.finally(() => clearTimeout(timeoutId)), 78: new Promise<never>((_, reject) => { 79: timeoutId = setTimeout(() => { 80: reject(new TimeoutError(message, ms)) 81: }, ms) 82: }), 83: ]) 84: } 1: interface NotifyBackendOptions { 2: preferCmux: boolean 3: tryCmuxNotify: () => Promise<boolean> 4: sendNodeNotify: () => void 5: } 6: 7: 66: }) 67: 68: const pendingSpawnSchema = z.object({ 69: branch: z.string().min(1), 70: path: z.string().min(1), 71: sessionId: z.string().min(1), 72: }) 73: 74: const pendingDeleteSchema = z.object({ 75: branch: z.string().min(1), 76: path: z.string().min(1), 77: }) 78: 79: // ============================================================================= 80: // DATABASE UTILITIES 81: // ============================================================================= 82: 83: /** 84: * Get the default base directory for worktree storage. 85: * Location: ~/.local/share/opencode/worktree/ 86: */ 87: function getWorktreeBaseDirectory(): string { 88: return path.join(os.homedir(), ".local", "share", "opencode", "worktree") 89: } 90: 91: /** 92: * Get the worktree path for a given project and branch. 93: * 94: * @param projectRoot - Absolute path to the project root 95: * @param branch - Branch name for the worktree 96: * @param basePath - Optional custom base path (absolute). Defaults to ~/.local/share/opencode/worktree 97: * @returns Absolute path to the worktree directory 98: */ 99: export async function getWorktreePath( 100: projectRoot: string, 101: branch: string, 102: basePath?: string, 103: ): Promise<string> { 104: if (!branch || typeof branch !== "string") { 105: throw new Error("branch is required") 106: } 107: const projectId = await getProjectId(projectRoot) 108: return path.join(basePath ?? getWorktreeBaseDirectory(), projectId, branch) 109: } 110: 111: /** 112: * Get the database directory path. 113: * Location: ~/.local/share/opencode/plugins/worktree/ 114: */ 115: function getDbDirectory(): string { 116: const home = os.homedir() 117: return path.join(home, ".local", "share", "opencode", "plugins", "worktree") 118: } 119: 120: /** 121: * Get the full database file path for a project. 122: * @param projectRoot - Absolute path to the project root 123: */ 124: async function getDbPath(projectRoot: string): Promise<string> { 125: const projectId = await getProjectId(projectRoot) 126: return path.join(getDbDirectory(), `${projectId}.sqlite`) 127: } 128: 129: /** 130: * Initialize the SQLite database for worktree state. 131: * Creates the database file and schema if they don't exist. 132: * 133: * @param projectRoot - Absolute path to the project root 134: * @returns Configured Database instance 135: * 136: * @example 137: * ```ts 138: * const db = await initStateDb("/home/user/my-project") 139: * const sessions = getAllSessions(db) 140: * db.close() 141: * ``` 142: */ 143: export async function initStateDb(projectRoot: string): Promise<Database> { 144: // Guard: validate project root 145: if (!projectRoot || typeof projectRoot !== "string") { 146: throw new Error("initStateDb requires a valid project root path") 147: } 148: 149: const dbPath = await getDbPath(projectRoot) 150: const dbDir = path.dirname(dbPath) 151: 152: // Create directory synchronously (required before opening DB) 153: mkdirSync(dbDir, { recursive: true }) 154: 155: // Open database (creates if doesn't exist) 156: const db = new Database(dbPath) 157: 158: // Configure SQLite for concurrent access 159: db.exec("PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL") 160: db.exec("PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000") 161: 162: // Create tables with schema 163: db.exec(` 164: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sessions ( 165: id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, 166: branch TEXT NOT NULL, 167: path TEXT NOT NULL, 168: created_at TEXT NOT NULL, 169: launch_mode TEXT, 170: profile TEXT, 171: ocx_bin TEXT 172: ) 173: `) 174: 175: ensureSessionLaunchMetadataColumns(db) 176: 177: db.exec(` 178: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS pending_operations ( 179: id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY CHECK (id = 1), 180: type TEXT NOT NULL, 181: branch TEXT NOT NULL, 182: path TEXT NOT NULL, 183: session_id TEXT 184: ) 185: `) 186: 187: return db 188: } 189: 190: function ensureSessionLaunchMetadataColumns(db: Database): void { 191: const tableInfo = db.prepare("PRAGMA table_info(sessions)").all() as Array<{ name?: string }> 192: const sessionColumns = new Set(tableInfo.map((column) => column.name).filter(Boolean)) 193: 194: if (!sessionColumns.has("launch_mode")) { 195: addSessionColumn(db, "launch_mode", "ALTER TABLE sessions ADD COLUMN launch_mode TEXT") 196: } 197: 198: if (!sessionColumns.has("profile")) { 199: addSessionColumn(db, "profile", "ALTER TABLE sessions ADD COLUMN profile TEXT") 200: } 201: 202: if (!sessionColumns.has("ocx_bin")) { 203: addSessionColumn(db, "ocx_bin", "ALTER TABLE sessions ADD COLUMN ocx_bin TEXT") 204: } 205: } 206: 207: function addSessionColumn(db: Database, columnName: string, sql: string): void { 208: try { 209: db.exec(sql) 210: } catch (error) { 211: if (isDuplicateColumnError(error, columnName)) { 212: return 213: } 214: 215: throw error 216: } 217: } 218: 219: function isDuplicateColumnError(error: unknown, columnName: string): boolean { 220: if (!(error instanceof Error)) { 221: return false 222: } 223: 224: const normalizedMessage = error.message.toLowerCase() 225: return ( 226: normalizedMessage.includes("duplicate column name") && 227: normalizedMessage.includes(columnName.toLowerCase()) 228: ) 229: } 230: 231: function normalizeSessionRow(row: Record<string, string | null>): Session { 232: const launchMetadata = parsePersistedLaunchMetadata({ 233: launchMode: row.launchMode, 234: profile: row.profile, 235: ocxBin: row.ocxBin, 236: }) 237: const serialized = serializePersistedLaunchMetadata(launchMetadata) 238: 239: return { 240: id: String(row.id), 241: branch: String(row.branch), 242: path: String(row.path), 243: createdAt: String(row.createdAt), 244: launchMode: serialized.launchMode, 245: profile: serialized.profile, 246: ocxBin: serialized.ocxBin, 247: } 248: } 249: 250: // ============================================================================= 251: // SESSION CRUD 252: // ============================================================================= 253: 254: /** 255: * Add a new session to the database. 256: * Uses atomic INSERT OR REPLACE for idempotency. 257: * 258: * @param db - Database instance from initStateDb 259: * @param session - Session data to persist 260: */ 261: export function addSession(db: Database, session: SessionInput): void { 262: // Parse at boundary for type safety 263: const parsed = sessionSchema.parse(session) 264: const launchMetadata = parsePersistedLaunchMetadata({ 265: launchMode: parsed.launchMode, 266: profile: parsed.profile, 267: ocxBin: parsed.ocxBin, 268: }) 269: const serializedLaunchMetadata = serializePersistedLaunchMetadata(launchMetadata) 270: 271: const stmt = db.prepare(` 272: INSERT OR REPLACE INTO sessions (id, branch, path, created_at, launch_mode, profile, ocx_bin) 273: VALUES ($id, $branch, $path, $createdAt, $launchMode, $profile, $ocxBin) 274: `) 275: 276: stmt.run({ 277: $id: parsed.id, 278: $branch: parsed.branch, 279: $path: parsed.path, 280: $createdAt: parsed.createdAt, 281: $launchMode: serializedLaunchMetadata.launchMode, 282: $profile: serializedLaunchMetadata.profile, 283: $ocxBin: serializedLaunchMetadata.ocxBin, 284: }) 285: } 286: 287: /** 288: * Get a session by ID. 289: * 290: * @param db - Database instance from initStateDb 291: * @param sessionId - Session ID to look up 292: * @returns Session if found, null otherwise 293: */ 294: export function getSession(db: Database, sessionId: string): Session | null { 295: // Guard: empty session ID 296: if (!sessionId) return null 297: 298: const stmt = db.prepare(` 299: SELECT id, branch, path, created_at as createdAt, launch_mode as launchMode, profile, ocx_bin as ocxBin 300: FROM sessions 301: WHERE id = $id 302: `) 303: 304: const row = stmt.get({ $id: sessionId }) as Record<string, string | null> | null 305: if (!row) return null 306: 307: return normalizeSessionRow(row) 308: } 309: 310: /** 311: * Remove a session by branch name. 312: * Deletes all sessions matching the branch. 313: * 314: * @param db - Database instance from initStateDb 315: * @param branch - Branch name to remove 316: */ 317: export function removeSession(db: Database, branch: string): void { 318: // Guard: empty branch 319: if (!branch) return 320: 321: const stmt = db.prepare(`DELETE FROM sessions WHERE branch = $branch`) 322: stmt.run({ $branch: branch }) 323: } 324: 325: /** 326: * Get all active sessions. 327: * 328: * @param db - Database instance from initStateDb 329: * @returns Array of all sessions, empty if none 330: */ 331: export function getAllSessions(db: Database): Session[] { 332: const stmt = db.prepare(` 333: SELECT id, branch, path, created_at as createdAt, launch_mode as launchMode, profile, ocx_bin as ocxBin 334: FROM sessions 335: ORDER BY created_at ASC 336: `) 337: 338: const rows = stmt.all() as Array<Record<string, string | null>> 339: return rows.map((row) => normalizeSessionRow(row)) 340: } 341: 342: // ============================================================================= 343: // PENDING SPAWN OPERATIONS 344: // ============================================================================= 345: 346: /** 347: * Set a pending spawn operation. Uses singleton pattern (last-write-wins). 348: * 349: * If a pending spawn already exists, it will be REPLACED and a warning logged. 350: * This is intentional: only the most recent spawn request should be processed. 351: * 352: * @param db - Database instance from initStateDb 353: * @param spawn - Spawn operation data 354: */ 355: export function setPendingSpawn(db: Database, spawn: PendingSpawn, client?: OpencodeClient): void { 356: // Parse at boundary for type safety 357: const parsed = pendingSpawnSchema.parse(spawn) 358: 359: // Check for existing operations and warn about replacement 360: const existingSpawn = getPendingSpawn(db) 361: const existingDelete = getPendingDelete(db) 362: 363: if (existingSpawn) { 364: logWarn( 365: client, 366: "worktree", 367: `Replacing pending spawn: "${existingSpawn.branch}" → "${parsed.branch}"`, 368: ) 369: } else if (existingDelete) { 370: logWarn( 371: client, 372: "worktree", 373: `Pending spawn replacing pending delete for: "${existingDelete.branch}"`, 374: ) 375: } 376: 377: // Atomic: replace any existing pending operation 378: const stmt = db.prepare(` 379: INSERT OR REPLACE INTO pending_operations (id, type, branch, path, session_id) 380: VALUES (1, 'spawn', $branch, $path, $sessionId) 381: `) 382: 383: stmt.run({ 384: $branch: parsed.branch, 385: $path: parsed.path, 386: $sessionId: parsed.sessionId, 387: }) 388: } 389: 390: /** 391: * Get the pending spawn operation if one exists. 392: * 393: * @param db - Database instance from initStateDb 394: * @returns PendingSpawn if exists and type is 'spawn', null otherwise 395: */ 396: export function getPendingSpawn(db: Database): PendingSpawn | null { 397: const stmt = db.prepare(` 398: SELECT type, branch, path, session_id as sessionId 399: FROM pending_operations 400: WHERE id = 1 AND type = 'spawn' 401: `) 402: 403: const row = stmt.get() as Record<string, string> | null 404: if (!row) return null 405: 406: return { 407: branch: row.branch, 408: path: row.path, 409: sessionId: row.sessionId, 410: } 411: } 412: 413: /** 414: * Clear any pending spawn operation. 415: * Removes the row if it's a spawn type, leaves deletes untouched. 416: * 417: * @param db - Database instance from initStateDb 418: */ 419: export function clearPendingSpawn(db: Database): void { 420: const stmt = db.prepare(`DELETE FROM pending_operations WHERE id = 1 AND type = 'spawn'`) 421: stmt.run() 422: } 423: 424: // ============================================================================= 425: // PENDING DELETE OPERATIONS 426: // ============================================================================= 427: 428: /** 429: * Set a pending delete operation. Uses singleton pattern (last-write-wins). 430: * 431: * If a pending delete already exists, it will be REPLACED and a warning logged. 432: * This is intentional: only the most recent delete request should be processed. 433: * 434: * @param db - Database instance from initStateDb 435: * @param del - Delete operation data 436: */ 437: export function setPendingDelete(db: Database, del: PendingDelete, client?: OpencodeClient): void { 438: // Parse at boundary for type safety 439: const parsed = pendingDeleteSchema.parse(del) 440: 441: // Check for existing operations and warn about replacement 442: const existingDelete = getPendingDelete(db) 443: const existingSpawn = getPendingSpawn(db) 444: 445: if (existingDelete) { 446: logWarn( 447: client, 448: "worktree", 449: `Replacing pending delete: "${existingDelete.branch}" → "${parsed.branch}"`, 450: ) 451: } else if (existingSpawn) { 452: logWarn( 453: client, 454: "worktree", 455: `Pending delete replacing pending spawn for: "${existingSpawn.branch}"`, 456: ) 457: } 458: 459: // Atomic: replace any existing pending operation 460: const stmt = db.prepare(` 461: INSERT OR REPLACE INTO pending_operations (id, type, branch, path, session_id) 462: VALUES (1, 'delete', $branch, $path, NULL) 463: `) 464: 465: stmt.run({ 466: $branch: parsed.branch, 467: $path: parsed.path, 468: }) 469: } 470: 471: /** 472: * Get the pending delete operation if one exists. 473: * 474: * @param db - Database instance from initStateDb 475: * @returns PendingDelete if exists and type is 'delete', null otherwise 476: */ 477: export function getPendingDelete(db: Database): PendingDelete | null { 478: const stmt = db.prepare(` 479: SELECT type, branch, path 480: FROM pending_operations 481: WHERE id = 1 AND type = 'delete' 482: `) 483: 484: const row = stmt.get() as Record<string, string> | null 485: if (!row) return null 486: 487: return { 488: branch: row.branch, 489: path: row.path, 490: } 491: } 492: 493: /** 494: * Clear any pending delete operation. 495: * Removes the row if it's a delete type, leaves spawns untouched. 496: * 497: * @param db - Database instance from initStateDb 498: */ 499: export function clearPendingDelete(db: Database): void { 500: const stmt = db.prepare(`DELETE FROM pending_operations WHERE id = 1 AND type = 'delete'`) 501: stmt.run() 502: } 1: /** 2: * Terminal Module for Worktree Plugin 3: * 4: * Provides mutex-protected tmux operations and cross-platform terminal spawning. 5: * Serializes tmux commands to prevent socket races since tmux server is single-threaded. 6: * 7: * This module is extracted from worktree.ts to provide a focused, testable 8: * interface for terminal operations with proper concurrency control. 9: */ 10: 11: import * as fs from "node:fs/promises" 12: import * as os from "node:os" 13: import * as path from "node:path" 14: import { z } from "zod" 15: import type { OpencodeClient } from "../kdco-primitives" 16: import { 17: escapeAppleScript, 18: escapeBash, 19: escapeBatch, 20: getTempDir, 21: isInsideTmux, 22: logWarn, 23: Mutex, 24: TimeoutError, 25: withTimeout, 26: } from "../kdco-primitives" 27: 28: // ============================================================================= 29: // TEMP SCRIPT HELPER 30: // ============================================================================= 31: 32: /** 33: * Execute a function with a temporary script file that is guaranteed to be cleaned up. 34: * Uses try-finally pattern to ensure cleanup even on errors. 35: * 36: * @param scriptContent - Content to write to the temp script 37: * @param fn - Function to execute with the script path 38: * @param extension - File extension for the script (default: ".sh") 39: * @returns Result of the function execution 40: */ 41: export async function withTempScript<T>( 42: scriptContent: string, 43: fn: (scriptPath: string) => Promise<T>, 44: extension: string = ".sh", 45: client?: OpencodeClient, 46: ): Promise<T> { 47: const scriptPath = path.join( 48: getTempDir(), 49: `worktree-${Date.now()}-${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2)}${extension}`, 50: ) 51: await Bun.write(scriptPath, scriptContent) 52: await fs.chmod(scriptPath, 0o755) 53: 54: try { 55: return await fn(scriptPath) 56: } finally { 57: try { 58: if (await Bun.file(scriptPath).exists()) { 59: await fs.rm(scriptPath) 60: } 61: } catch (cleanupError) { 62: // Log but don't throw - cleanup is best-effort 63: logWarn(client, "worktree", `Failed to cleanup temp script: ${scriptPath}: ${cleanupError}`) 64: } 65: } 66: } 67: 68: /** 69: * Wrap a bash script with trap-based self-cleanup. 70: * The script deletes itself on ANY exit (success, error, or signal). 71: * This eliminates race conditions with detached processes. 72: */ 73: function wrapWithSelfCleanup(script: string): string { 74: return `#!/bin/bash 75: trap 'rm -f "$0"' EXIT INT TERM 76: ${script}` 77: } 78: 79: /** 80: * Wrap a batch script with self-cleanup. 81: * Uses goto trick to delete itself after execution. 82: */ 83: function wrapBatchWithSelfCleanup(script: string): string { 84: return `@echo off 85: ${script} 86: (goto) 2>nul & del "%~f0"` 87: } 88: 89: /** Build Warp launch configuration YAML for Linux. */ 90: function buildWarpLaunchConfigYaml( 91: name: string, 92: cwd: string, 93: configPath: string, 94: command?: string, 95: ): string { 96: const quotedName = JSON.stringify(name) 97: const quotedCwd = JSON.stringify(cwd) 98: const cleanupCommand = `rm -f "${escapeBash(configPath)}"` 99: const commands = [cleanupCommand] 100: if (command) { 101: commands.push(command) 102: } 103: const commandsBlock = `\n commands:${commands 104: .map((cmd) => `\n - exec: ${JSON.stringify(cmd)}`) 105: .join("")}` 106: 107: return `--- 108: name: ${quotedName} 109: active_window_index: 0 110: windows: 111: - active_tab_index: 0 112: tabs: 113: - layout: 114: cwd: ${quotedCwd}${commandsBlock} 115: ` 116: } 117: 118: /** Get Warp launch configuration directory for current platform user. */ 119: function getWarpLaunchConfigDir(): string { 120: const xdgDataHome = process.env.XDG_DATA_HOME ?? path.join(os.homedir(), ".local", "share") 121: return path.join(xdgDataHome, "warp-terminal", "launch_configurations") 122: } 123: 124: // ============================================================================= 125: // TYPES 126: // ============================================================================= 127: 128: /** Terminal type for the current platform */ 129: export type TerminalType = "tmux" | "cmux" | "macos" | "windows" | "linux-desktop" 130: 131: /** Result of a terminal operation */ 132: export interface TerminalResult { 133: success: boolean 134: error?: string 135: } 136: 137: function normalizeArgv(argv?: string[]): string[] { 138: if (!argv) { 139: return [] 140: } 141: 142: return argv 143: } 144: 145: export function buildBashCommandFromArgv(argv?: string[]): string | undefined { 146: const normalizedArgv = normalizeArgv(argv) 147: if (normalizedArgv.length === 0) { 148: return undefined 149: } 150: 151: return normalizedArgv.map((arg) => `"${escapeBash(arg)}"`).join(" ") 152: } 153: 154: export function buildBatchCommandFromArgv(argv?: string[]): string | undefined { 155: const normalizedArgv = normalizeArgv(argv) 156: if (normalizedArgv.length === 0) { 157: return undefined 158: } 159: 160: return normalizedArgv.map((arg) => `"${escapeBatch(arg).replace(/"/g, '""')}"`).join(" ") 161: } 162: 163: type ResolveExecutable = (command: string) => string | null | undefined 164: type CmuxEnvironment = Record<string, string | undefined> 165: type CmuxCommandResult = { 166: exitCode: number 167: stderr: string 168: } 169: type RunCmuxCommand = (args: string[]) => CmuxCommandResult | Promise<CmuxCommandResult> 170: 171: export interface CmuxTerminalExecutionResult { 172: terminalResult: TerminalResult 173: hasStateMutation: boolean 174: } 175: 176: const CMUX_COMMAND_TIMEOUT_MS = 1500 177: 178: // Singleton mutex for all tmux operations in this process 179: const tmuxMutex = new Mutex() 180: 181: /** Stabilization delay after spawning tmux windows (ms) */ 182: const STABILIZATION_DELAY_MS = 150 183: 184: // ============================================================================= 185: // ENVIRONMENT DETECTION SCHEMAS 186: // ============================================================================= 187: 188: /** Validates WSL environment detection */ 189: const wslEnvSchema = z.object({ 190: WSL_DISTRO_NAME: z.string().optional(), 191: WSLENV: z.string().optional(), 192: }) 193: 194: /** Validates cmux environment detection */ 195: const cmuxEnvSchema = z.object({ 196: CMUX_WORKSPACE_ID: z.string().optional(), 197: CMUX_SURFACE_ID: z.string().optional(), 198: CMUX_SOCKET_PATH: z.string().optional(), 199: CMUX_SOCKET_MODE: z.string().optional(), 200: }) 201: 202: /** Validates Linux terminal environment detection */ 203: const linuxTerminalEnvSchema = z.object({ 204: KITTY_WINDOW_ID: z.string().optional(), 205: WEZTERM_PANE: z.string().optional(), 206: ALACRITTY_WINDOW_ID: z.string().optional(), 207: GHOSTTY_RESOURCES_DIR: z.string().optional(), 208: TERM_PROGRAM: z.string().optional(), 209: GNOME_TERMINAL_SERVICE: z.string().optional(), 210: KONSOLE_VERSION: z.string().optional(), 211: }) 212: 213: /** Environment variables for macOS terminal detection */ 214: const macTerminalEnvSchema = z.object({ 215: TERM_PROGRAM: z.string().optional(), 216: GHOSTTY_RESOURCES_DIR: z.string().optional(), 217: ITERM_SESSION_ID: z.string().optional(), 218: KITTY_WINDOW_ID: z.string().optional(), 219: ALACRITTY_WINDOW_ID: z.string().optional(), 220: __CFBundleIdentifier: z.string().optional(), 221: }) 222: 223: type LinuxTerminal = 224: | "kitty" 225: | "wezterm" 226: | "alacritty" 227: | "ghostty" 228: | "warp" 229: | "foot" 230: | "gnome-terminal" 231: | "konsole" 232: | "xfce4-terminal" 233: | "xdg-terminal-exec" 234: | "x-terminal-emulator" 235: | "xterm" 236: 237: type MacTerminal = "ghostty" | "iterm" | "warp" | "kitty" | "alacritty" | "terminal" 238: 239: // ============================================================================= 240: // PLATFORM DETECTION 241: // ============================================================================= 242: 243: /** 244: * Check if running inside WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux). 245: * Checks environment variables and os.release() for Microsoft string. 246: */ 247: function isInsideWSL(): boolean { 248: const parsed = wslEnvSchema.safeParse(process.env) 249: if (parsed.success && (parsed.data.WSL_DISTRO_NAME || parsed.data.WSLENV)) { 250: return true 251: } 252: 253: // Fallback: check os.release() for Microsoft string 254: try { 255: return os.release().toLowerCase().includes("microsoft") 256: } catch { 257: return false 258: } 259: } 260: 261: export interface CmuxContext { 262: workspaceID?: string 263: surfaceID?: string 264: socketPath?: string 265: socketMode?: string 266: } 267: 268: function normalizeCmuxValue(value?: string): string | undefined { 269: const trimmed = value?.trim() 270: return trimmed ? trimmed : undefined 271: } 272: 273: export function detectCmuxContext(env: CmuxEnvironment = process.env): CmuxContext { 274: const parsed = cmuxEnvSchema.parse(env) 275: 276: return { 277: workspaceID: normalizeCmuxValue(parsed.CMUX_WORKSPACE_ID), 278: surfaceID: normalizeCmuxValue(parsed.CMUX_SURFACE_ID), 279: socketPath: normalizeCmuxValue(parsed.CMUX_SOCKET_PATH), 280: socketMode: normalizeCmuxValue(parsed.CMUX_SOCKET_MODE), 281: } 282: } 283: 284: export function canUseCmuxWorkflow( 285: env: CmuxEnvironment = process.env, 286: resolveExecutable: ResolveExecutable = (command) => Bun.which(command), 287: cmuxExecutable: string = "cmux", 288: ): boolean { 289: if (!resolveExecutable(cmuxExecutable)) { 290: return false 291: } 292: 293: const context = detectCmuxContext(env) 294: if (context.workspaceID) { 295: return true 296: } 297: 298: const socketModeAllowsExternalControl = context.socketMode?.toLowerCase() === "allowall" 299: return Boolean(context.socketPath && socketModeAllowsExternalControl) 300: } 301: 302: type PlatformTerminalType = Exclude<TerminalType, "tmux" | "cmux"> 303: 304: function detectPlatformTerminalType(): PlatformTerminalType { 305: // WSL check (Linux inside Windows) - before platform detection 306: if (process.platform === "linux" && isInsideWSL()) { 307: return "windows" // Use Windows Terminal via interop 308: } 309: 310: // Platform-specific 311: switch (process.platform) { 312: case "darwin": 313: return "macos" 314: case "win32": 315: return "windows" 316: case "linux": 317: return "linux-desktop" 318: default: 319: return "linux-desktop" 320: } 321: } 322: 323: /** 324: * Detect the best terminal type for the current platform. 325: * Priority: tmux > cmux > WSL/platform-specific 326: * 327: * @returns The detected terminal type 328: */ 329: export function detectTerminalType(): TerminalType { 330: // tmux takes priority - user may be inside tmux on any platform 331: if (isInsideTmux()) { 332: return "tmux" 333: } 334: 335: if (canUseCmuxWorkflow()) { 336: return "cmux" 337: } 338: 339: return detectPlatformTerminalType() 340: } 341: 342: // ============================================================================= 343: // TMUX OPERATIONS (MUTEX-PROTECTED) 344: // ============================================================================= 345: 346: /** 347: * Open a new tmux window with mutex protection. 348: * Includes stabilization delay after spawning to prevent races. 349: * 350: * SECURITY NOTE: Branch names and paths are passed via array-based spawn 351: * (Bun.spawnSync with array arguments), NOT shell string interpolation. 352: * This prevents command injection even if values contain special characters. 353: * The tmux `-n` flag treats its argument as a literal window name string. 354: * 355: * @param options - Window configuration 356: * @param options.sessionName - Optional tmux session name (uses current session if not specified) 357: * @param options.windowName - Name for the new window 358: * @param options.cwd - Working directory for the window 359: * @param options.command - Optional command to execute in the window 360: * @returns Success status and optional error message 361: * 362: * @example 363: * ```ts 364: * const result = await openTmuxWindow({ 365: * windowName: "feature-branch", 366: * cwd: "/path/to/worktree", 367: * command: "opencode --session abc123", 368: * }) 369: * if (!result.success) { 370: * console.error(result.error) 371: * } 372: * ``` 373: */ 374: export async function openTmuxWindow(options: { 375: sessionName?: string 376: windowName: string 377: cwd: string 378: argv?: string[] 379: }): Promise<TerminalResult> { 380: const { sessionName, windowName, cwd, argv } = options 381: const command = buildBashCommandFromArgv(argv) 382: 383: return tmuxMutex.runExclusive(async () => { 384: try { 385: // Build tmux new-window command 386: const tmuxArgs = ["new-window", "-n", windowName, "-c", cwd, "-P", "-F", "#{pane_id}"] 387: 388: // Add session target if specified 389: if (sessionName) { 390: tmuxArgs.splice(1, 0, "-t", sessionName) 391: } 392: 393: // If there's a command to run, create script first and pass it to new-window 394: if (command) { 395: const scriptPath = path.join(getTempDir(), `worktree-${Bun.randomUUIDv7()}.sh`) 396: const escapedCwd = escapeBash(cwd) 397: const scriptContent = wrapWithSelfCleanup( 398: `cd "${escapedCwd}" || exit 1 399: ${command} 400: exec $SHELL`, 401: ) 402: await Bun.write(scriptPath, scriptContent) 403: Bun.spawnSync(["chmod", "+x", scriptPath]) 404: 405: // Add script execution to tmux args 406: tmuxArgs.push("--", "bash", scriptPath) 407: } 408: 409: const createResult = Bun.spawnSync(["tmux", ...tmuxArgs]) 410: 411: if (createResult.exitCode !== 0) { 412: return { 413: success: false, 414: error: `Failed to create tmux window: ${createResult.stderr.toString()}`, 415: } 416: } 417: 418: // Stabilization delay to let tmux server process the window 419: await Bun.sleep(STABILIZATION_DELAY_MS) 420: 421: return { success: true } 422: } catch (error) { 423: return { 424: success: false, 425: error: error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error), 426: } 427: } 428: }) 429: } 430: 431: // ============================================================================= 432: // CMUX OPERATIONS 433: // ============================================================================= 434: 435: export function buildCmuxCommandSequence( 436: _context: CmuxContext, 437: cwd: string, 438: argv?: string[], 439: ): string[][] { 440: // Product policy: each worktree launch gets a new cmux workspace. 441: // We intentionally do not reuse the current workspace context. 442: const cmuxArgs = ["new-workspace", "--cwd", cwd] 443: const command = buildBashCommandFromArgv(argv) 444: 445: if (command) { 446: cmuxArgs.push("--command", command) 447: } 448: 449: return [cmuxArgs] 450: } 451: 452: async function runCmuxCommandWithBun( 453: cmuxCommand: string, 454: args: string[], 455: ): ["ignore", "ignore", "ignore"], 1256: }) 1257: proc.unref() 1258: return { success: true } 1259: } catch { 1260: // Fall through to bash 1261: } 1262: } 1263: 1264: // Fallback: open in current terminal (new bash process) 1265: try { 1266: const proc = Bun.spawn(["bash", scriptPath], { 1267: cwd, 1268: detached: true, 1269: stdio: ["ignore", "ignore", "ignore"], 1270: }) 1271: proc.unref() `Failed to spawn terminal: ${error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)}`, 1289: } 1290: } 1291: } 1292: 1293: //
