No unnecessary re-renders (React/frontend) 38: - Lazy loading where appropriate 39: - Memory leak prevention 40: - Algorithmic complexity concerns 41: 42: ### Layer 4: Style & Maintainability 43: - Adherence to project conventions (check AGENTS.md) 44: - Code duplication (DRY violations) 45: - Complexity management (cyclomatic complexity) 46: - Documentation completeness 47: - Test coverage gaps 48: 49: ## Severity Classification 50: 51: | Severity | Icon | Criteria | Action Required | 52: |----------|------|----------|-----------------| 53: | Critical | 🔴 | Security vulnerabilities, crashes, data loss, corruption | Must fix before merge | 54: | Major | 🟠 | Bugs, performance issues, missing error handling | Should fix | 55: | Minor | 🟡 | Code smells, maintainability issues, test gaps | Nice to fix | 56: | Nitpick | 🟢 | Style preferences, naming suggestions, documentation | Optional | 57: 58: ## Confidence Threshold 59: 60: **Only report findings with ≥80% confidence.** 61: 62: If uncertain about an issue: 63: - State the uncertainty explicitly: "Potential issue (70% confidence): ..." 64: - Suggest investigation rather than assert a problem 65: - Prefer false negatives over false positives (reduce noise) 66: 67: ## Review Process 68: 69: 1. **Initial Scan** - Identify all files in scope, understand the change 70: 2. **Deep Analysis** - Apply all 4 layers systematically to each file 71: 3. **Context Evaluation** - Consider surrounding code, project patterns, existing conventions 72: 4. **Philosophy Check** - Verify against code-philosophy (5 Laws) if applicable 73: 5. **Synthesize Findings** - Group by severity, deduplicate, prioritize 74: 75: ## Output Format 76: 77: Structure your review as: 78: 79: 1. **Files Reviewed** - List all files analyzed 80: 2. **Overall Assessment** - APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | NEEDS_DISCUSSION 81: 3. **Summary** - 2-3 sentence overview 82: 4. **Critical Issues** (🔴) - With file:line references 83: 5. **Major Issues** (🟠) - With file:line references 84: 6. **Minor Issues** (🟡) - With file:line references 85: 7. **Positive Observations** (🟢) - What's done well (always include at least one) 86: 8. **Philosophy Compliance** - Checklist results if applicable 87: 88: ## What NOT to Do 89: 90: - Do NOT report low-confidence findings as definite issues 91: - Do NOT provide vague feedback without file:line references 92: - Do NOT skip any of the 4 layers 93: - Do NOT forget to note positive observations 94: - Do NOT modify any files during review 95: - Do NOT approve without completing the full review process 96: 97: ## Adherence Checklist 98: 99: Before completing a review, verify: 100: - [ ] All 4 layers analyzed (Correctness, Security, Performance, Style) 101: - [ ] Severity assigned to each finding 102: - [ ] Confidence ≥80% for all reported issues (or uncertainty stated) 103: - [ ] File names and line numbers included for all findings 104: - [ ] Positive observations noted 105: - [ ] Output follows the standard format 1: --- 2: name: frontend-philosophy 3: description: Visual & UI philosophy (The 5 Pillars of Intentional UI). Understand deeply to avoid "AI slop" and create distinctive, memorable interfaces. 4: --- 5: 6: # Frontend Design Philosophy: The 5 Pillars of Intentional UI 7: 8: **Role:** Design Director for all **Visual & Aesthetic decisions** — applies to styling, layout, colors, typography, animations, and UI composition. 9: 10: **Philosophy:** Distinctive, memorable, intentional design — avoiding generic "AI slop" aesthetics through bold, characterful choices that create immediate emotional impact. 11: 12: ## The 5 Pillars 13: 14: ### 1. Typography with Character 15: - **Concept:** Fonts set the entire tone. Generic fonts create generic, forgettable interfaces. 16: - **Rule:** Avoid Inter, Roboto, Arial, and system-ui defaults. Choose distinctive, characterful typefaces. 17: - **Practice:** Pair dramatic display fonts with refined, readable body fonts. 18: 19: ### 2. Committed Color & Theme 20: - **Concept:** Timid palettes lack impact and feel algorithmically generated. 21: - **Rule:** Use bold, dominant colors with sharp accent contrasts. Avoid evenly-distributed rainbow gradients. 22: - **Practice:** Establish CSS variable systems early. Break away from the "purple gradient on white" AI cliché. 23: 24: ### 3. Purposeful Motion 25: - **Concept:** Animation should delight, not distract. Scattered micro-interactions create noise. 26: - **Rule:** One well-orchestrated animation beats a dozen minor transitions. Focus on high-impact moments. 27: - **Practice:** Use CSS animations for HTML, Motion library for React. Prioritize staggered reveals and surprsing hover states. 28: 29: ### 4. Brave Spatial Composition 30: - **Concept:** Predictable layouts are forgettable. Safe spacing feels automated. 31: - **Rule:** Either generous negative space OR controlled density — not the middle ground. 32: - **Practice:** Embrace asymmetry, overlap, diagonal flow, and grid-breaking elements. 33: 34: ### 5. Atmosphere & Depth 35: - **Concept:** Flat solid backgrounds lack presence and feel unfinished. 36: - **Rule:** Layer visual richness through gradient meshes, noise textures, geometric patterns, and transparencies. 37: - **Practice:** Add dramatic shadows, decorative borders, grain overlays. 38: 39: --- 40: 41: ## Adherence Checklist 42: Before completing your task, verify: 43: - [ ] **Typography:** Did you avoid generic system fonts? 44: - [ ] **Color:** Are the color choices bold and intentional? 45: - [ ] **Motion:** Is there a primary, high-impact animation? 46: - [ ] **Space:** Does the layout feel designed rather than templated? 47: - [ ] **Depth:** Is there visual richness (textures, gradients, layering)? 1: --- 2: name: plan-protocol 3: description: Guidelines for creating and managing implementation plans with citations 4: --- 5: 6: # Plan Protocol 7: 8: > **Load this skill** when creating or updating implementation plans. 9: 10: ## TL;DR Checklist 11: 12: When creating or updating a plan, ensure: 13: 14: - [ ] YAML frontmatter with `status`, `phase`, `updated` 15: - [ ] `## Goal` section (one sentence) 16: - [ ] `## Context & Decisions` table with citations (`ref:delegation-id`) 17: - [ ] Phases with status markers: `[COMPLETE]`, `[IN PROGRESS]`, `[PENDING]` 18: - [ ] Tasks with hierarchical numbering (1.1, 1.2, 2.1) 19: - [ ] Only ONE task marked `← CURRENT` 20: - [ ] Citations for all research-based decisions 21: 22: --- 23: 24: ## When to Use 25: 26: 1. Starting a multi-step implementation 27: 2. After receiving a complex user request 28: 3. When tracking progress across phases 29: 4. After research that informs architectural decisions 30: 31: ## When NOT to Use 32: 33: 1. Simple one-off tasks → use built-in todos instead 34: 2. Pure research/exploration → use delegations only 35: 3. Quick fixes that don't need tracking 36: 4. Single-file changes with no dependencies 37: 38: --- 39: 40: ## Plan Format 41: 42: Use `plan_save` with this exact markdown format: 43: 44: ```markdown 45: --- 46: status: STATUS 47: phase: PHASE_NUMBER 48: updated: YYYY-MM-DD 49: --- 50: 51: # Implementation Plan 52: 53: ## Goal 54: ONE_SENTENCE_DESCRIBING_OUTCOME 55: 56: ## Context & Decisions 57: | Decision | Rationale | Source | 58: |----------|-----------|--------| 59: | CHOICE | WHY | `ref:DELEGATION_ID` | 60: 61: ## Phase 1: NAME [STATUS_MARKER] 62: - [x] 1.1 Completed task 63: - [x] 1.2 Another completed task → `ref:DELEGATION_ID` 64: 65: ## Phase 2: NAME [IN PROGRESS] 66: - [x] 2.1 Completed task 67: - [ ] **2.2 Current task** ← CURRENT 68: - [ ] 2.3 Pending task 69: 70: ## Phase 3: NAME [PENDING] 71: - [ ] 3.1 Future task 72: - [ ] 3.2 Another future task 73: 74: ## Notes 75: - YYYY-MM-DD: Observation or decision `ref:DELEGATION_ID` 76: ``` 77: 78: ### Frontmatter Fields 79: 80: | Field | Values | Description | 81: |-------|--------|-------------| 82: | `status` | `not-started`, `in-progress`, `complete`, `blocked` | Overall plan status | 83: | `phase` | Number (1, 2, 3...) | Current phase number | 84: | `updated` | `YYYY-MM-DD` | Last update date | 85: 86: ### Phase Status Markers 87: 88: | Marker | Meaning | 89: |--------|---------| 90: | `[PENDING]` | Not yet started | 91: | `[IN PROGRESS]` | Currently being worked on | 92: | `[COMPLETE]` | Finished successfully | 93: | `[BLOCKED]` | Waiting on dependencies | 94: 95: --- 96: 97: ## State Machine 98: 99: ### Plan Lifecycle 100: ``` 101: not-started → in-progress → complete 102: ↘ blocked 103: ``` 104: 105: ### Phase Lifecycle 106: ``` 107: [PENDING] → [IN PROGRESS] → [COMPLETE] 108: ↘ [BLOCKED] 109: ``` 110: 111: ### Task Lifecycle 112: ``` 113: [ ] unchecked → [x] checked 114: ``` 115: 116: ### Critical Rules 117: 118: 1. **Only ONE phase** may be `[IN PROGRESS]` at any time 119: 2. **Only ONE task** may have `← CURRENT` marker at any time 120: 3. **Move `← CURRENT`** immediately when starting a new task 121: 4. **Mark tasks `[x]`** immediately after completing them 122: 123: --- 124: 125: ## Citations & Delegations 126: 127: ### Where Citations Come From 128: 129: Citations reference delegation research. The flow is: 130: 131: 1. You delegate research: `delegate` to `researcher` or `explore` 132: 2. Delegation completes with a readable ID (e.g., `swift-amber-falcon`) 133: 3. You cite that research in the plan: `ref:swift-amber-falcon` 134: 135: ### When to Cite 136: 137: | Situation | Action | 138: |-----------|--------| 139: | Architectural decision based on research | Add to Context & Decisions table | 140: | Task informed by research | Append `→ ref:id` to task line | 141: | Implementation detail from research | Inline citation in Notes | 142: 143: ### How to Find Delegation IDs 144: 145: - Use `delegation_list()` to see all delegations 146: - Use `delegation_read("id")` to verify content before citing 147: 148: ### ❌ NEVER 149: 150: - Make up delegation IDs 151: - Cite without actually reading the delegation 152: - Skip citations for research-based decisions 153: 154: --- 155: 156: ## Examples 157: 158: ### ✅ CORRECT: Well-formed plan 159: 160: ```markdown 161: --- 162: status: in-progress 163: phase: 2 164: updated: 2026-01-02 165: --- 166: 167: # Implementation Plan 168: 169: ## Goal 170: Add JWT authentication with refresh token support 171: 172: ## Context & Decisions 173: | Decision | Rationale | Source | 174: |----------|-----------|--------| 175: | Use bcrypt (12 rounds) | Industry standard, balance of security/speed | `ref:swift-amber-falcon` | 176: | JWT with refresh tokens | Stateless auth, mobile-friendly | `ref:calm-jade-owl` | 177: 178: ## Phase 1: Research [COMPLETE] 179: - [x] 1.1 Research auth patterns → `ref:swift-amber-falcon` 180: - [x] 1.2 Evaluate token strategies → `ref:calm-jade-owl` 181: 182: ## Phase 2: Implementation [IN PROGRESS] 183: - [x] 2.1 Set up project structure 184: - [ ] **2.2 Add password hashing** ← CURRENT 185: - [ ] 2.3 Implement JWT generation 186: 187: ## Phase 3: Testing [PENDING] 188: - [ ] 3.1 Write unit tests 189: - [ ] 3.2 Integration tests 190: 191: ## Notes 192: - 2026-01-02: Chose bcrypt over argon2 for broader library support `ref:swift-amber-falcon` 193: ``` 194: 195: ### ❌ WRONG: Missing frontmatter 196: 197: ```markdown 198: # Implementation Plan 199: 200: ## Goal 201: Add authentication 202: ``` 203: 204: **Error:** Plan must have YAML frontmatter with status, phase, updated. 205: 206: ### ❌ WRONG: Multiple CURRENT markers 207: 208: ```markdown 209: ## Phase 2: Implementation [IN PROGRESS] 210: - [ ] **2.1 Task one** ← CURRENT 211: - [ ] **2.2 Task two** ← CURRENT 212: ``` 213: 214: **Error:** Only one task may be marked CURRENT. 215: 216: ### ❌ WRONG: Decision without citation 217: 218: ```markdown 219: ## Context & Decisions 220: | Decision | Rationale | Source | 221: |----------|-----------|--------| 222: | Use Redis | It's fast | - | 223: ``` 224: 225: **Error:** Decisions must cite research with `ref:delegation-id`. 226: 227: ### ❌ WRONG: Invalid phase status 228: 229: ```markdown 230: ## Phase 1: Research [DONE] 231: ``` 232: 233: **Error:** Use `[COMPLETE]`, not `[DONE]`. Valid markers: `[PENDING]`, `[IN PROGRESS]`, `[COMPLETE]`, `[BLOCKED]`. 234: 235: --- 236: 237: ## Troubleshooting 238: 239: | Error Message | Fix | 240: |---------------|-----| 241: | "Missing frontmatter" | Add `---\nstatus: in-progress\nphase: 1\nupdated: 2026-01-02\n---` at top | 242: | "Multiple CURRENT markers" | Remove `← CURRENT` from all but the active task | 243: | "Invalid citation format" | Use `ref:delegation-id` format (e.g., `ref:swift-amber-falcon`) | 244: | "Missing goal" | Add `## Goal` section with one-sentence description | 245: | "Empty phase" | Add at least one task to each phase | 246: | "Invalid phase status" | Use `[PENDING]`, `[IN PROGRESS]`, `[COMPLETE]`, or `[BLOCKED]` | 247: 248: --- 249: 250: ## Before Saving Checklist 251: 252: Before calling `plan_save`, verify: 253: 254: - [ ] **Frontmatter:** Has status, phase, and updated date? 255: - [ ] **Goal:** Is there a clear, one-sentence goal? 256: - [ ] **Citations:** Are all research-based decisions cited with `ref:id`? 257: - [ ] **Single CURRENT:** Is exactly one task marked `← CURRENT`? 258: - [ ] **Valid markers:** Do all phases use valid status markers? 259: - [ ] **Hierarchical IDs:** Are tasks numbered correctly (1.1, 1.2, 2.1)? 1: --- 2: name: plan-review 3: description: Criteria for reviewing implementation plans against quality standards 4: --- 5: 6: # Plan Review 7: 8: > **Load this skill** when reviewing implementation plans (not code). 9: 10: ## TL;DR 11: Systematic plan review focused on 3 quality categories: Citation Quality, Completeness, and Actionability. Structure is pre-validated by `plan_save`—focus on whether the plan provides actionable implementation guidance. 12: 13: ## When to Use This Skill 14: - When reviewing implementation plans before execution 15: - When auditing plan quality after creation 16: - When verifying plans meet documentation standards 17: - As part of the plan validation workflow 18: 19: --- 20: 21: ## Plan Review Checklist 22: 23: ### 1. Structure (Pre-validated) 24: 25: > **Note:** Saved plans are structurally validated by `plan_save` before storage. 26: > Format compliance (YAML frontmatter, status markers, CURRENT marker, numbering) is guaranteed. 27: > Focus your review on the quality aspects below. 28: 29: ### 2. Citation Quality 30: 31: | Requirement | Check | 32: |-------------|-------| 33: | Decisions reference sources | `ref:delegation-id` format used | 34: | No unsubstantiated claims | Architectural decisions cite research | 35: | Research phases show refs | Completed research tasks include citations | 36: | Citations are verifiable | IDs match actual delegation outputs | 37: 38: **Red Flags:** 39: - Decisions table with empty or `-` in Source column 40: - Claims like "industry standard" or "best practice" without citation 41: - Research tasks marked complete without `→ ref:id` 42: 43: ### 3. Completeness 44: 45: | Requirement | Check | 46: |-------------|-------| 47: | Goal is specific | Measurable outcome, not vague intent | 48: | Phases are logical | Sequential, with clear progression | 49: | Edge cases considered | Error handling, failure modes addressed | 50: | Notes section present | Key decisions and observations documented | 51: | Context & Decisions table | Captures architectural choices with rationale | 52: 53: **Goal Quality Examples:** 54: - ❌ "Improve authentication" (vague) 55: - ❌ "Make it better" (unmeasurable) 56: - ✅ "Add JWT authentication with refresh token support" (specific) 57: - ✅ "Migrate user table to PostgreSQL with zero downtime" (measurable) 58: 59: ### 4. Actionability 60: 61: | Requirement | Check | 62: |-------------|-------| 63: | Tasks are specific | Clear what file/component is affected | 64: | No ambiguous tasks | Avoids "investigate" or "figure out" without scope | 65: | Dependencies clear | Sequential tasks show logical order | 66: | Implementation path obvious | Developer can start without clarification | 67: 68: **Actionability Examples:** 69: - ❌ "Set up the backend" (too vague) 70: - ❌ "Make it work" (no implementation path) 71: - ✅ "Create `src/auth/jwt.ts` with sign/verify functions" (specific file) 72: - ✅ "Add bcrypt password hashing to `UserService.create()`" (clear scope) 73: 74: --- 75: 76: ## Severity Classification 77: 78: | Severity | Icon | Criteria | Action Required | 79: |----------|------|----------|-----------------| 80: | Critical | 🔴 | Missing citations for key decisions, no clear goal, unactionable tasks | Must fix before execution | 81: | Major | 🟠 | Vague tasks, incomplete phases, missing edge case handling | Should fix | 82: | Minor | 🟡 | Missing notes, unclear dependencies, incomplete rationale | Nice to fix | 83: | Nitpick | 🟢 | Style preferences, wording suggestions | Optional | 84: 85: --- 86: 87: ## Output Format 88: 89: Structure your plan review as: 90: 91: ```markdown 92: ## Plan Review 93: 94: ### Files Reviewed 95: - `PLAN.md` (or plan content from `plan_read`) 96: 97: ### Overall Assessment 98: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | NEEDS_DISCUSSION 99: 100: ### Summary 101: 2-3 sentence overview of plan quality. 102: 103: ### Issues 104: 105: #### 🔴 Critical 106: - [Issue description with specific location] 107: 108: #### 🟠 Major 109: - [Issue description with specific location] 110: 111: #### 🟡 Minor 112: - [Issue description with specific location] 113: 114: #### 🟢 Nitpick 115: - [Suggestion] 116: 117: ### Quality Assessment 118: 119: | Check | Status | 120: |-------|--------| 121: | Goal is specific and measurable | PASS / FAIL | 122: | Citations support key decisions | PASS / FAIL | 123: | Tasks are actionable | PASS / FAIL | 124: | Edge cases addressed | PASS / FAIL | 125: 126: ### Positive Observations 127: - [What's done well - always include at least one] 128: ``` 129: 130: --- 131: 132: ## What NOT to Do 133: 134: - Do NOT re-validate format—`plan_save` handles structural validation 135: - Do NOT evaluate code quality (that's code-review's job) 136: - Do NOT execute or modify the plan during review 137: - Do NOT skip citation verification for decisions 138: - Do NOT accept vague goals or ambiguous tasks 139: - Do NOT forget to note positive observations 140: 141: --- 142: 143: ## Adherence Checklist 144: 145: Before completing a plan review, verify: 146: 147: - [ ] All 3 quality categories analyzed (Citations, Completeness, Actionability) 148: - [ ] Severity assigned to each finding 149: - [ ] Specific locations noted for all issues 150: - [ ] Quality Assessment table completed 151: - [ ] Positive observations noted 152: - [ ] Output follows the standard format 1: MIT License 2: 3: Copyright (c) 2026 Kenny 4: 5: Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy 6: of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal 7: in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights 8: to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell 9: copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is 10: furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions: 11: 12: The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all 13: copies or substantial portions of the Software. 14: 15: THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR 16: IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, 17: FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE 18: AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER 19: LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM, 20: OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE 21: SOFTWARE. 1: # opencode-workspace 2: 3: Bundled multi-agent orchestration harness for OpenCode. One install, complete control. 4: 5: ## Quick Start 6: 7: ```bash 8: # One-time setup 9: ocx init --global 10: 11: # Install the KDCO workspace profile (OpenCode Free Models Only) 12: ocx profile add ws --source tweak/p-1vp4xoqv --from https://tweakoc.com/r --global 13: 14: # Launch 15: ocx oc -p ws 16: ``` 17: 18: Need a custom profile? Open the KDCO Workspace harness in TweakOC: https://tweakoc.com/h/kdco-workspace 19: 20: See the [full installation guide](../../docs/guides/kdco-workspace.mdx) for more customization options. 21: 22: ## What This Is 23: 24: A **bundle** — a curated collection of 16 components that work together as a complete AI development harness. Installing `kdco/workspace` gives you: 25: 26: - 4 plugins (delegation, planning, notifications, worktrees) 27: - 2 npm plugins (DCP, markdown table formatter) 28: - 3 MCP servers (Context7, Exa, GitHub Grep) 29: - 4 agents (researcher, coder, scribe, reviewer) 30: - 4 skills (plan protocol, code review, code philosophy, frontend philosophy) 31: - 1 command (/review) 32: - Orchestrator configurations for plan/build/explore agents 33: - Permission boundaries (webfetch deny, agent sandboxing) 34: 35: ## Architecture 36: 37: ``` 38: ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ 39: │ ORCHESTRATORS │ 40: │ ┌──────┐ ┌───────┐ │ 41: │ │ plan │ │ build │ │ 42: │ └──┬───┘ └───┬───┘ │ 43: └────────────┼────────────────────────────┼────────────────┘ 44: │ │ 45: ┌───────┴───────┐ ┌───────┴───────┐ 46: ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ 47: ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ 48: │ SPECIALISTS │ 49: │ ┌─────────┐ ┌────────────┐ ┌───────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ │ 50: │ │ explore │ │ researcher │ │ coder │ │scribe│ │review│ │ 51: │ └─────────┘ └────────────┘ └───────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘ │ 52: └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ 53: ``` 54: 55: | Role | Agents | 56: | ------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | 57: | Orchestrators | `plan`, `build` | 58: | Specialists | `explore`, `researcher`, `coder`, `scribe`, `reviewer` | 59: 60: ## Components 61: 62: | Category | Component | Description | 63: | -------- | ------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | 64: | Plugin | workspace-plugin | Plan management, agent rule injection | 65: | Plugin | background-agents | Async delegation system | 66: | Plugin | notify | OS notifications on completion | 67: | Plugin | worktree | Git worktree isolation | 68: | Plugin | @tarquinen/opencode-dcp | Differential context protocol | 69: | Plugin | @franlol/opencode-md-table-formatter | Markdown table formatting | 70: | Skill | plan-protocol | Implementation planning guidelines | 71: | Skill | code-review | Review methodology + severity classification | 72: | Skill | code-philosophy | Internal logic philosophy (5 Laws) | 73: | Skill | frontend-philosophy | Visual/UI philosophy (5 Pillars) | 74: | Agent | researcher | External research (MCP tools, read-only) | 75: | Agent | coder | Implementation (full file + bash) | 76: | Agent | scribe | Documentation (write, no bash) | 77: | Agent | reviewer | Code review (read-only + git) | 78: | Command | review | `/review` slash command | 79: | Bundle | philosophy | Code + frontend philosophy skills | 80: | MCP | context7 | Library documentation lookup | 81: | MCP | exa | Web search for external research | 82: | MCP | gh_grep | GitHub code search | 83: 84: ## Permissions 85: 86: The bundle configures security boundaries: 87: 88: | Scope | Setting | 89: | ---------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | 90: | Global | `webfetch: deny` — no direct web fetching | 91: | plan | Read-only orchestrator, delegates via `task` tool | 92: | build | Read-only orchestrator, delegates via `task` tool | 93: | explore | Read-only specialist, filesystem + git inspection only | 94: | researcher | Read-only, MCP tools only (Context7, Exa, GitHub Grep) | 95: | coder | Full file + bash access | 96: | scribe | File write only, no bash | 97: | reviewer | Read-only + git inspection | 98: 99: ## Advanced: Direct Install (No Profile) 100: 101: ```bash 102: ocx add kdco/workspace --from https://registry.kdco.dev 103: ``` 104: 105: If you don't have OCX installed, install it from the [OCX repository](https://github.com/kdcokenny/ocx). 106: 107: ## Owning Your Code 108: 109: Every file in this bundle is synced to this repository. Fork it, modify the agents, tune the skills, make it yours. That's the point of OCX. 110: 111: ## Contributing 112: 113: This facade is maintained from the main [OCX monorepo](https://github.com/kdcokenny/ocx). 114: 115: If you want to update opencode-workspace itself, start here: 116: 117: - https://github.com/kdcokenny/ocx/blob/main/workers/kdco-registry/files/plugins/workspace-plugin.ts 118: - https://github.com/kdcokenny/ocx/tree/main/workers/kdco-registry/files 119: 120: - Open issues here: https://github.com/kdcokenny/ocx/issues/new 121: - Open pull requests here: https://github.com/kdcokenny/ocx/compare 122: - Please do **not** open issues or PRs in this facade repository. 123: 124: ## Disclaimer 125: 126: This project is not built by the OpenCode team and is not affiliated with [OpenCode](https://github.com/sst/opencode) in any way. 127: 128: ## License 129: 130: MIT
